claim: Agents are non-deterministic in production; you must manage this non-determinism through evaluate-monitor-optimize loops, not treat it as just a demo problem
actionable: Design your agent evaluation and monitoring strategy from day one to detect and respond to drift caused by model changes, prompt tweaks, and edge cases accumulation
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Evaluations should be trace-linked: run evals at multiple points in the agent workflow (intent resolution, tool call, final response) rather than just once at the end
actionable: Instrument your agent to capture evaluation results at each step (intent check → tool selection → output quality) so failures pinpoint exactly where optimization is needed
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Task adherence is the metric that drifts most over time and needs continuous tweaking; groundedness and relevance are often secondary concerns
actionable: Prioritize task adherence in your evaluation suite and monitor it continuously; use it as your primary signal for prompt/workflow drift
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Red teaming (automated adversarial testing) is distinct from safety evaluation; safety evals check guardrail compliance on normal queries, red teaming proactively finds prompt injection and jailbreak vectors
actionable: Run both safety evaluations (passive guardrail checks) and red teaming scans (active attacks like leetspeak, crescendo, string reversal) to catch vulnerabilities guardrails miss
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Crescendo attacks (gradually escalating requests) and leetspeak attacks expose vulnerabilities that simple guardrails miss; run them in red teaming to find multi-stage jailbreak vectors
actionable: Use red teaming frameworks (e.g., PAIR) that test crescendo attacks and obfuscation (leetspeak, reversal) rather than relying on single-prompt safety checks
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Tracing built on OpenTelemetry (via Azure Application Insights) allows agents built with different frameworks to be observed centrally, making it framework-agnostic
actionable: Instrument agents with OpenTelemetry traces and export to Application Insights or Azure Monitor for unified observability across heterogeneous agent deployments
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Workflow agents (declarative multi-agent composition via YAML) decouple specialist agents (flight, hotel, car agents) and enable cost/performance per-agent optimization without rebuilding monoliths
actionable: Break monolithic agents into specialist workflow agents (YAML-defined DAGs); optimize only underperforming agents and roll back individual versions independently
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Coding agents (AI-driven prompt optimization) with human-in-the-loop can automatically generate eval datasets, run batch evals, optimize prompts, compare versions, and roll back to the best version from a single instruction
actionable: Use coding agents (via GitHub Copilot skills or equivalent) to automate the observe loop: generate eval data → run batch evals → optimize prompts → compare → roll back, with human approval at decision points
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: When prompt optimization regresses on some metrics while improving others, human-in-the-loop rollback to the best prior version (by overall score) prevents local optima traps
actionable: Implement version tracking and rollback logic in your optimization loop; if a new version scores 7/10 but the previous was 8/10, roll back and try a different optimization direction
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Custom trace attributes (logging domain-specific metadata into traces) enable faster debugging by surfacing exactly what went wrong in production without needing to reconstruct state
actionable: Add custom attributes to your traces (e.g., user_intent_confidence, tool_call_latency, guardrail_triggered) to make diagnosis faster when evals flag failures
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Code Spaces with dev containers eliminates local environment setup friction, allowing developers to jump straight into agent development without installing dependencies or tools
actionable: Use dev containers (e.g., GitHub Code Spaces) to provision cloud-hosted VS Code with all dependencies pre-installed; uncheck main branch when forking to get all workshop branches
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Intent resolution, tool call correctness, and task adherence are the three key agentic evaluation points; focus on these for quality at the agent level (not just model-level evals)
actionable: Build evaluation datasets with test cases that measure intent resolution (did the agent understand?), tool call correctness (did it call the right tool?), and task adherence (did it complete the task?)
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft

claim: Starting with portal-based agent creation before jumping to SDK code reduces cognitive load and lets you understand built-in evaluators before writing custom ones
actionable: Build your first agent in the Foundry portal (add instructions, tools, select metrics), test it, then replicate in SDK code; this sequence de-risks decision paralysis with 11,000+ available models
source_title: Mind the Gap (In your Agent Observability) — Amy Boyd & Nitya Narasimhan, Microsoft
